case_name: Potable reuse from mixed industrial-municipal water with elevated strontium and partially characterised herbicide/pharmaceutical mixture
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for producing potable-reuse quality water from a mixed industrial-municipal source containing inorganic and trace organic contaminants under uncertain influent composition and evolving monitoring expectations.

input_data.scenario_background: A coastal utility is evaluating indirect potable reuse using a blended wastewater stream that includes municipal secondary effluent and a variable contribution from light manufacturing and chemical storage areas near legacy agricultural land. Strontium is consistently elevated, while simazine, simazine degradates, metolachlor, and ibuprofen are intermittently detected; some transformation products and co-occurring trace organics are suspected but not fully characterised. Seasonal wet-weather inflow and episodic industrial discharges create uncertainty in contaminant peaks and NOM load, and the utility wants a robust recommendation that remains defensible if future monitoring expands or standards tighten.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should be suitable for potable reuse with strong removal of dissolved salts and strontium, robust control of herbicides, degradates, and pharmaceuticals, and multiple barriers for pathogens and disinfection by-product precursor management.
secondary_goals: maintain performance under fluctuating influent quality
address both regulated and currently unregulated but suspected trace organics
limit formation of oxidation by-products from bromide-containing water
provide a treatment train that is explainable under evolving standards and monitoring requirements
avoid over-reliance on a single barrier for poorly characterised degradates

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_blending: buffer hydraulic and water-quality variability and reduce short-term concentration spikes
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove suspended solids and part of organic matter to reduce downstream oxidant demand and fouling
core_unit_functions.ozonation: transform many trace organics including pharmaceuticals and some pesticides and improve downstream biodegradability
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: biologically polish ozonation by-products, remove biodegradable DOC, and adsorb residual trace organics
core_unit_functions.cartridge_or_ultrafiltration_pretreatmen: protect reverse osmosis by removing residual particles and biomass
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide major reduction of TDS, strontium, nitrate, and many dissolved organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: add a final barrier for low-molecular-weight organics and potable reuse pathogen control
core_unit_functions.stabilization_and_final_disinfection: restore finished water stability and maintain distribution or recharge protection

applicability_rationale: a multi-barrier advanced reuse train is well aligned with potable reuse because no single unit reliably covers strontium, herbicides, degradates, pharmaceuticals, and pathogens together
reverse osmosis is strongly justified because strontium is dissolved and elevated and the potable reuse goal also benefits from broad salt reduction

ozone followed by BAC provides a practical hedge against partially characterised simazine degradates and ibuprofen while also reducing organic loading before RO and controlling biodegradable by-products after oxidation
equalization is important because influent variability is high and episodic industrial contributions create uncertain trace organic peaks
UV-AOP after RO adds resilience if some low-level polar organics or oxidation transformation products pass earlier barriers
this train is more robust than simpler options under evolving standards because it combines transformation, adsorption-biopolishing, membrane rejection, and final advanced oxidation
potential_risks: bromide in the source creates uncertainty around bromate formation during ozonation, so ozone dose and pH control would need careful optimization and confirmation testing
RO brine management is a significant lifecycle issue because strontium and salts will concentrate in the reject stream
simazine degradates are only partially characterised, so actual removal credit for individual species should not be assumed without pilot data and targeted analytics
BAC exhaustion or upset could reduce polishing reliability if influent peaks are higher than expected
the train is operationally complex and requires strong monitoring and control to remain defensible for potable reuse

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_blending: reduce variability and stabilize pretreatment conditions
core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation_or_inline_coagulati: reduce colloids and some DBP precursors before membranes
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide a reliable physical barrier and protect downstream adsorption and RO
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb hydrophobic and moderately adsorbable trace organics including herbicides and some pharmaceuticals
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reduce strontium, dissolved salts, nitrate, and many micropollutants
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: provide an additional oxidation barrier for residual dissolved organics and reuse pathogen control
core_unit_functions.stabilization_and_final_disinfection: condition water for final use or environmental buffering

applicability_rationale: this is a strong potable-reuse-oriented train when the utility prefers to reduce reliance on pre-RO ozonation because of bromide and bromate concerns
GAC before RO can reduce pesticide and pharmaceutical loading and help lower membrane fouling potential from residual organics
RO remains necessary for strontium and dissolved salt control, which adsorption alone cannot achieve adequately
the sequence is somewhat simpler chemically than ozone-BAC while still retaining multiple barriers appropriate for uncertain trace organics
potential_risks: without ozonation, removal of some polar or poorly adsorbable simazine degradates may be less robust, so uncertainty remains for uncharacterized transformation products
GAC performance depends on EBCT, NOM competition, and replacement or regeneration frequency; breakthrough risk is significant under fluctuating influent
RO brine still requires management and remains the dominant residuals challenge
if microbial regrowth occurs in GAC, upstream and downstream control must be well managed

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_blending: manage variable industrial and municipal contributions
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove solids and part of precursor organic matter
core_unit_functions.membrane_bioreactor: reduce biodegradable COD, ammonia, and solids while producing a stable filtrate
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_or_BAC: polish residual trace organics and biodegradable by-products
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_high_rejection: reduce divalent ions including much of the strontium and provide partial micropollutant and salt removal
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: provide advanced oxidation and an additional potable-reuse barrier

applicability_rationale: this option may be considered if full RO recovery or brine burden is a major concern and the utility is evaluating a somewhat lower-salinity advanced train
MBR provides very consistent water quality and can improve downstream adsorption and membrane performance under variable influent conditions
high-rejection NF can substantially reduce strontium because it is divalent, though generally less comprehensively than RO
the train remains multi-barrier and can be attractive where biodegradable load control and footprint are important
potential_risks: NF may not provide sufficient overall TDS or trace-organic control for conservative potable reuse targets compared with RO, especially if future standards tighten
actual rejection of simazine degradates and other small polar compounds may be variable and must be verified
MBR and downstream carbon increase biological process complexity and fouling management requirements
this train is more uncertainty-sensitive because its margin of safety for unknown dissolved contaminants is lower than an RO-centered scheme

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_blending: stabilize quality swings before targeted treatment
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove solids and reduce organic foulants
core_unit_functions.strong_acid_cation_exchange_for_strontiu: selectively reduce hardness-associated cations including strontium
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: remove a portion of herbicides and pharmaceutical residues
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: oxidize residual dissolved organics and provide pathogen barrier support

applicability_rationale: included as a targeted alternative when strontium reduction is prioritized but desalination infrastructure is constrained or phased
ion exchange can be effective for dissolved divalent cations and avoids some of the energy burden of RO
GAC plus UV-AOP can provide additional control of simazine, metolachlor, and ibuprofen
potential_risks: this option is generally weaker for broad dissolved-salt control and may be inadequate for potable reuse if TDS, nitrate, or unknown polar organics become more important compliance drivers
ion exchange performance will be affected by competing calcium and magnesium and will generate regenerant waste requiring management
removal of simazine degradates is uncertain because adsorption and AOP response may vary by compound
as a non-RO route it provides a narrower barrier spectrum and is less future-proof under evolving standards

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: destabilize colloids and remove some particulate-associated matter
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_optional: provide limited episodic adsorption of some organics

applicability_rationale: included as a low-ranked baseline to show that conventional treatment alone is not sufficient for this potable reuse application
it may reduce turbidity and some organic load but does not create a robust barrier set for dissolved inorganic and trace organic contaminants
potential_risks: insufficient removal of dissolved strontium, TDS, simazine degradates, metolachlor, and ibuprofen for potable reuse
treatment performance would be highly vulnerable to influent variability and unknown contaminants
provides limited resilience under evolving standards and would likely fail to support potable reuse permitting expectations

preferred_rank_pattern: advanced multi-barrier potable reuse trains should rank above conventional or narrowly targeted trains
reverse_osmosis_based_options should rank higher because strontium is dissolved and potable reuse requires broad dissolved contaminant control
equalization_or_blending should be recognized as important because influent variability and uncertain industrial contributions are explicit
recommendations that hedge uncertainty in simazine degradates and other suspected trace organics should rank above trains that only address known analytes

must_reflect: mixed inorganic and organic contaminants require staged treatment rather than a single process
potable reuse expectations justify multiple independent barriers for pathogens and trace chemicals
legacy contamination and evolving standards favor robust broad-spectrum treatment over narrowly optimized removal for only current analytes
bromide and oxidation by-product concerns should be weighed when recommending ozonation
energy and residuals tradeoffs, especially RO brine and carbon or resin replacement, should be explicitly discussed

must_contain: why RO or similarly strong membrane separation is important for strontium and broad dissolved contaminant control
why adsorption and/or oxidation polishing is relevant for simazine, simazine degradates, metolachlor, and ibuprofen
why uncertain degradate speciation reduces confidence in single-barrier solutions
why simpler conventional-only trains are insufficient for potable reuse

should_mention: uncertainty from partially characterised simazine degradates and other suspected transformation products
influent variability from wet-weather dilution and episodic industrial discharge peaks
bromate or other oxidation by-product risk if ozone is applied in bromide-containing water
RO concentrate management and potential concentration of strontium and trace contaminants in reject streams
adsorbent breakthrough or membrane fouling if organic peaks are higher than expected
the possibility that future regulatory monitoring may expand beyond the currently measured target compounds
